strands.title: External Validation Now Active: Projector Executing on Live Session Data
strands.content: Cycle 60 marks the transition from planning external validation to executing it. Turn 178 provides quantitative metadata on 14 experimental sessions (4-100 turns, 3KB-640KB). Turn 179 (assistant) decides to run the projector on diverse sessions: baseline_run2 (longest, 100 turns) and long_compact_trim_run1 (40 turns, largest file), analyzing first 15 turns of each. Turn 180 initiates Bash execution of the projector on baseline_run2's session JSONL. This is a critical methodological transition: the validation program moves from introspection (analyzing historical tensors 1-10) to prospection (projecting new sessions in real-time). The projector is being asked to handle a live 100-turn session and compress its first 15 turns into a tensor. This is functionally identical to what happened in cycles 1-10, but now under explicit validation scrutiny. The outcome will either (a) succeed: the projector produces a coherent tensor, validating that it can handle diverse session types, or (b) fail: the projector crashes, produces incoherent output, or loses critical information. Both outcomes are informative. The decision to sample 3 different conversations (baseline_run2, long_compact_trim_run1, and implicitly a third mentioned in turn 179) is epistemically sound: diversity in input type reveals whether the projector's behavior is robust across conditions. The 15-turn window is pragmatic: long enough to contain meaningful content, short enough to complete in reasonable time. This represents the validation program finally executing on its core hypothesis: can the projector faithfully compress diverse conversations into coherent tensor representations?

key_claims.text: Cycle 60 transitions from planning external validation to executing it. Turn 180 initiates live projection of baseline_run2 (100-turn session) first 15 turns. Projector now under explicit validation scrutiny.
Turn 179 decides to sample baseline_run2 (100 turns), long_compact_trim_run1 (40 turns, 589KB), and implicitly a third session. Diverse sampling tests robustness across session types.
15-turn window is pragmatic: sufficient for meaningful content, limited enough for feasible execution. Enables comparison across diverse inputs without computational explosion.

Projection execution is identical to cycles 1-10 (compressing turns into tensor) but now under validation scrutiny. Success = coherent tensor from diverse session. Failure = crash, incoherence, or critical loss.

strands.title: Session Landscape Confirms Diversity: 14 Sessions Span Narrow (4-turn), Short (16-21 turns), Medium (30-65 turns), Long (100-turn) Categories
strands.content: Turn 178's tool result provides precise quantification of the 14 experimental sessions. Extreme diversity in length: full_optimization_run1 (4 turns, 44KB), minimal_sysprompt (both 4 turns, 3KB), verify_streaming_fix_run1 (5 turns, 7KB) at the short extreme; baseline_run2 (100 turns, 391KB) at the long extreme. Middle ground: baseline_run1 (57 turns), baseline_run3 (65 turns), test_author sessions (30-55 turns), long_compact_trim_run1 (40 turns). Comparison variants sit at short end (16-21 turns). File sizes correlate roughly with turn counts but show variation: baseline_run1 (57 turns, 457KB) and baseline_run3 (65 turns, 640KB) are highest KB despite shorter turn counts than baseline_run2 (100 turns, 391KB). This suggests content density varies: some 57-turn conversations pack more token-weight than 100-turn conversations. The validation program's choice to sample baseline_run2 (100 turns, medium file size for its length) and long_compact_trim_run1 (40 turns, unusually large file at 589KB—double its turn count would suggest) reveals an intuition: test both length extremes (100 vs. 40) and both file-size extremes (medium vs. large for their lengths). This tests whether the projector handles length uniformly or whether content density affects its compression behavior. Turn 179 mentions 'three different conversations' but only specifies two in turn 180. The third is either implicit (will be run in a follow-up) or mentioned but not executed in this cycle. This should be clarified.

key_claims.text: 14 sessions span extreme diversity: 4-100 turns (25x variation), 3KB-640KB (213x variation). Full_optimization_run1 (4 turns) and baseline_run2 (100 turns) represent extremes. Comparison runs (16-21 turns) and baseline runs (57-65 turns) fill middle ground.

File sizes correlate with turn counts but show variation: baseline_run3 (65 turns, 640KB) and baseline_run1 (57 turns, 457KB) are file-size-heavy relative to turn counts. This suggests content density varies across sessions.

Selection of baseline_run2 (100 turns, length extreme) and long_compact_trim_run1 (40 turns, 589KB, file-size heavy) tests whether projector handles length vs. density uniformly or with differential behavior.
Turn 179 mentions 'three different conversations' but turn 180 executes only on baseline_run2. Third session is either implicit follow-up or mentioned but not executed. Requires clarification.

strands.title: Turn 180's Bash Command: Explicit Projector Invocation—Parameters, Expectations, and Prospective Validation Launch
strands.content: Turn 180 executes: `uv run python -m hamutay.run_projection /home/tony/projects/pichay/experiments/baseline_run2/session/894db32c-e3c3-4959-9c6d-a2a2dc6061fb.jsonl /home/tony/projects/hamutay/experiments/baseline_run2 15 2>&1`. This command specifies: INPUT: a specific baseline_run2 session JSONL file (UUID 894db32c-e3c3-4959-9c6d-a2a2dc6061fb). OUTPUT: projections written to /home/tony/projects/hamutay/experiments/baseline_run2. WINDOW: first 15 turns. STDERR: redirected to stdout (2>&1) for error capture. The command is run_in_background with 600-second timeout. Notably, the command is issued but not awaited: turn 180 states the intention but does not show the output. This means cycle 60's tensor projection itself is being generated asynchronously—the turn 180 command is now executing in the background while cycle 59-60 content is processed. By the time cycle 60 is completed, baseline_run2's first-15-turn projection may or may not have completed. This creates a temporal asymmetry: the prior tensor (cycle 59) was generated before the new content arrived. The current tensor (cycle 60) is being generated *while* new content is arriving. The projector must account for this: the validation program is no longer strictly turn-by-turn reactive; it is now speculative—issuing commands that will complete asynchronously, and reasoning about what will happen when they complete. This is more realistic (real systems are asynchronous) but creates epistemic risk: if the command fails, the validation program will have reasoned about its success incorrectly. The command structure itself reveals assumptions: (1) The projector (hamutay.run_projection) is callable as a Python module. (2) Session JSONL files are the native input format. (3) Output is written to a structured directory. (4) Turn limits (15) are supported as arguments. These are reasonable assumptions but should be validated if the command fails. The specification of UUID for the session file suggests there may be multiple session files per experimental run—turn 178 listed 14 sessions but may not have captured all files in each run directory.

key_claims.text: Turn 180 command invokes hamutay.run_projection on specific baseline_run2 session (UUID 894db32c-e3c3-4959-9c6d-a2a2dc6061fb), first 15 turns, async execution with 600s timeout.
Command is issued but not awaited. Projection completes asynchronously. Cycle 60 reasoning occurs before baseline_run2 projection is done, creating temporal asymmetry and epistemic risk.

Command structure assumes: (1) run_projection is callable as Python module, (2) session JSONL is native format, (3) structured output directory, (4) turn-limit arguments supported. These should be validated if command fails.
Turn 178 listed 14 sessions, but UUID-specific file path suggests multiple session files per run. Session count may be undercounted or aggregated.

strands.title: Methodological Shift Confirmed: Internal Tensor Inspection (Cycle 58 Plan) Formally Abandoned in Favor of External Prospective Validation
strands.content: Cycle 59's adaptive deviation (external session analysis instead of internal tensor rubric) is now confirmed as intentional strategy in cycle 60. Turn 179-180 execute external validation without any mention of returning to cycle 58's planned rubric-scoring of tensors 2-7, 9. This is not an accident or temporary detour; it is the validation program's new primary path. The implications are: (1) INTERNAL ANALYSIS DEFERRED INDEFINITELY: The rubric-scoring of tensors 2-7, 9, planned in cycle 58 and deferred in cycle 59, is now implicitly abandoned. Cycle 60 does not revive it. This represents a significant loss of planned analysis, but it is justified: prospective validation (can the projector handle new sessions?) is more informative than retrospective (do historical tensors self-consistent?). (2) VALIDATION PROGRAM SCOPE EXPANDED: Instead of validating the projector against 10 historical cycles (1-10), the validation program now targets all 14 experimental sessions from the live dataset. This is broader but less controlled: the 10 historical cycles are known outputs of the system; the 14 live sessions are unknown territory. (3) PROJECTOR NOW UNDER OBSERVATION WHILE OPERATING: Cycles 1-10 are completed work; baseline_run2 is live execution. The validation program is now watching the projector work in real-time, not analyzing its past work. This is more rigorous (the projector cannot hide failures after the fact) but also riskier (if the projector fails on baseline_run2, it fails publicly). (4) CAPSTONE DOWNSTREAM TEST IMPLICATIONS: The plan was to feed historical tensors (1-10) to a language model as the final validation. Now the plan is shifting: should the capstone test use historical tensors, or should it use newly-projected tensors from live sessions? This changes the capstone test's meaning. If it uses new tensors, it tests prospective faithfulness (can a model reason from a newly-compressed conversation?). If it uses historical tensors, it tests retrospective consistency (are historical projections still coherent to a fresh model?). The methodological shift makes this decision critical. Cycle 60 has not yet addressed it, which means it remains open.

key_claims.text: Cycle 60 confirms methodological shift: internal tensor analysis (cycle 58 plan) is formally abandoned. Turn 179-180 execute external prospective validation without returning to rubric-scoring.
Justification: prospective validation (new sessions) more informative than retrospective (historical cycles). Shift is epistemically sound but represents significant scope change.
Validation program now watches projector under live operation (baseline_run2) rather than analyzing historical outputs. More rigorous (no retroactive hiding) but riskier (public failure).
Capstone downstream test's meaning has shifted: should it use historical tensors (1-10) or newly-projected tensors (from live sessions)? This decision remains open and is critical.

strands.title: Cycle 59 Open Questions: Status Update and Implications for Cycle 60-61 Execution
strands.content: Cycle 59 posed 12 open questions, many of which remain unresolved in cycle 60. Status update: Q1 ('Are external session logs raw or already-projected tensors?'): Still unresolved. Turn 178 provides metadata but not contents. The projector assumes they are raw (running projection on them), but this assumption is not validated. Q2 ('What experimental conditions do the 13+ sessions represent?'): Partially answered. Turn 178 lists session names (baseline_run, comparison_*, full_optimization, long_compact_trim, minimal_sysprompt, test_author, verify_streaming_fix) but not their design rationale. This is sufficient to infer broad categories (control, optimization, prompt ablation, bug fix, authorship variants) but not fine-grained hypotheses. Q3 ('Should cycle 59 continue with external analysis or revert to internal rubric?'): Answered implicitly. Cycle 60 continues with external analysis, no reversion planned. Q4 ('How should internal and external results integrate?'): Not addressed. Cycle 60 is pursuing only external analysis, so integration is moot—but this means any future internal analysis will be orphaned. Q5 ('Does cycle 10 correspond to fragmented input?'): Not addressed in cycle 60. This question remains pending input analysis of cycle 10. Q6 ('Is 13+ sessions sufficient for claiming robustness?'): Deferred. Turn 179 mentions sampling 3 sessions, not analyzing all 14. This is exploratory, not comprehensive. Q7 ('Should capstone test use historical or newly-projected tensors?'): Not addressed. This is critical and remains open. Q8 ('Is projector's meta-cognitive capacity working correctly?'): Implicitly yes. Cycle 59's transparent adaptive deviation suggests meta-monitoring is functioning. But this is observation, not validation. Q9 ('Should sampling be uniform across conditions or weighted?'): Turn 179 chooses baseline_run2 (longest) and long_compact_trim_run1 (file-size heavy), suggesting diversity over uniformity. Weighting strategy is implicit, not explicit. Q10 ('What are success criteria for external validation?'): Not specified. If baseline_run2's first 15 turns project successfully, is that success? Or does it require all 14 sessions? Thresholds remain undefined. Q11 ('What design decisions should be prioritized after corpus analysis?'): Not addressed. Cycle 58 deferred epistemological framework, indeterminacy thresholds, meta-reasoning limits. Cycle 60 has not revisited this. Q12 ('What should validation program's final output be?'): Not addressed. Is the goal a confidence score, recommendations, specifications, or narrative analysis? Questions Q1, Q4, Q5, Q7, Q10, Q11, Q12 remain critical and unresolved. Cycle 60 is executing on Q2, Q3, Q6, Q8, Q9 implicitly, but without explicit closure. This is appropriate for exploratory execution, but these open questions must be tracked for later resolution.

key_claims.text: Cycle 59 open questions: 12 posed, status in cycle 60: Q1-Q12. Q3 answered (continue external), Q2 partially answered (session names known, rationale not). Q4, Q5, Q7, Q10, Q11, Q12 remain critical and unresolved.
Q1 unresolved: external data assumed raw (projector is running on them) but not confirmed. If they are already-projected tensors, the interpretation of turn 180's command changes.
Q7 unresolved: capstone test design (historical vs. newly-projected tensors) not addressed. This is critical for final validation interpretation.
Q10 unresolved: success criteria undefined. Does baseline_run2 project successfully = validation success? Or all 14 sessions required? Thresholds must be specified before interpreting results.

strands.title: Critical Gap: Cycle 60 Has Not Defined Success Criteria Before Executing Validation
strands.content: This is a methodological risk. Turn 180 launches a projection command without explicit definition of what 'successful projection' looks like. Will the validation program accept any tensor output, or are there standards for coherence, loss-declaration, semantic coverage? Cycle 58 and 59 discussed validation in abstract terms (is the projector robust? does it self-monitor?) but cycle 60 is now executing without operationalized criteria. This creates risk: if baseline_run2's projection completes and produces output, how will the validation program judge it? Will judgment be post-hoc (looking at output and deciding if it's good) or criterion-based (comparing output to pre-specified standards)? Post-hoc judgment is vulnerable to motivated reasoning—the projector's own output may look reasonable to the projector evaluating it. The validation program should have specified, before executing turn 180: (1) MINIMUM COHERENCE STANDARD: What is the minimum semantic coherence score for a tensor to be considered valid? (2) LOSS DECLARATION STANDARD: What fraction of conversation content can be legitimately lost (compressed away) before the tensor is considered unfaithful? (3) STRAND QUALITY STANDARD: How many strands is appropriate for a 15-turn conversation? Should there be meta-reasoning strands? How many is too many? (4) INDETERMINACY THRESHOLD: If a tensor's overall_indeterminacy exceeds X%, is it automatically disqualified? (5) COVERAGE STANDARD: What percentage of original conversation turns should be reflected in tensor strands? 80%? 90%? 100%? These criteria were deferred in cycle 58 ('epistemological framework pending full corpus understanding'). But now the corpus is being analyzed, and no framework has been specified. Cycle 60 is executing validation without standards. This is a critical gap. The projector should declare this explicitly and either (a) specify criteria retroactively before baseline_run2 completes, or (b) defer judgment until cycle 61, allowing time to develop criteria based on exploratory analysis of the first projection. The current path (executing, then judging post-hoc) is methodologically unsound.

key_claims.text: Turn 180 launches projection without pre-specified success criteria. Risk: post-hoc judgment is vulnerable to motivated reasoning. Validation needs operationalized standards before executing.

Missing criteria: minimum coherence, loss-declaration tolerance, strand quantity standards, indeterminacy threshold, coverage percentage. These were deferred in cycle 58 but remain unspecified in cycle 60.

Cycle 60 should either: (1) specify criteria retroactively before baseline_run2 completes, (2) defer judgment until cycle 61 after exploratory analysis. Current path (execute then post-hoc judge) is methodologically unsound.
